tasks.title: Warm-up: Set up the project and load documents
tasks.prompt: Create a project folder called `rag-mvp`, install the required packages, and write `ingest.py` so that it loads all `.pdf`, `.md`, and `.txt` files from a `data/` sub-folder using LangChain document loaders. Print the total number of `Document` fragments loaded and the metadata of the first one to confirm everything works.
tasks.starter_code:
# ingest.py  (fill in the blanks)
from pathlib import Path
from langchain_community.document_loaders import (
    DirectoryLoader,
    ___,          # loader class for PDF files
    ___,          # loader class for plain text / markdown
)

DATA_DIR = Path("data")

# TODO: create three DirectoryLoader instances (pdf, md, txt)
# TODO: call .load() on each and concatenate the results
docs = []
print(f"Loaded {len(docs)} document fragments")
if docs:
    print("Sample metadata:", docs[0].metadata)
tasks.solution:
# ingest.py
from pathlib import Path
from langchain_community.document_loaders import (
    DirectoryLoader,
    PyPDFLoader,
    TextLoader,
)

DATA_DIR = Path("data")

pdf_loader = DirectoryLoader(
    str(DATA_DIR),
    glob="**/*.pdf",
    loader_cls=PyPDFLoader,
)
md_loader = DirectoryLoader(
    str(DATA_DIR),
    glob="**/*.md",
    loader_cls=TextLoader,
    loader_kwargs={"encoding": "utf-8"},
)
txt_loader = DirectoryLoader(
    str(DATA_DIR),
    glob="**/*.txt",
    loader_cls=TextLoader,
    loader_kwargs={"encoding": "utf-8"},
)

docs = pdf_loader.load() + md_loader.load() + txt_loader.load()
print(f"Loaded {len(docs)} document fragments")
if docs:
    print("Sample metadata:", docs[0].metadata)
tasks.explanation: Each `DirectoryLoader` is told (a) the root folder, (b) a glob pattern to select file types, and (c) which concrete loader class to delegate to. `PyPDFLoader` returns one `Document` per PDF page and automatically stamps `source` and `page` into `metadata`. `TextLoader` handles both `.md` and `.txt` because Markdown is plain text at the loader level. Concatenating the three `.load()` results gives a single flat list regardless of how many files or file types exist in `data/`. Printing `metadata` of the first fragment lets you confirm the `source` key is present — you will need that key for source citations later.
hints: The loader class for PDFs is `PyPDFLoader`; for plain text and Markdown it is `TextLoader`. Both live in `langchain_community.document_loaders`.
Pass `loader_kwargs={"encoding": "utf-8"}` when constructing `DirectoryLoader` for text files so non-ASCII characters in your documents don't cause a decode error.

Call `loader.load()` on each of the three loaders and join the three lists with `+`. If `docs` is still empty, double-check that the files are actually inside the `data/` folder and that the glob patterns match their extensions.

tasks.title: Core 1: Chunk the documents and ingest into Chroma
tasks.prompt: Continuing `ingest.py`, split the loaded documents into chunks using `RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter` (chunk size 1000, overlap 150), then embed the chunks with `text-embedding-3-small` and persist them into a local Chroma collection called `rag_mvp` at `./chroma_db`. Print the total number of chunks indexed. Make sure the `.env` file with `OPENAI_API_KEY` is loaded before the embedding call.
tasks.starter_code:
# add to ingest.py (after the loading section from Task 1)
from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
from langchain_chroma import Chroma
from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv()

# TODO: create the splitter with chunk_size=1000, chunk_overlap=150
#       and separators=["\n\n", "\n", ". ", " ", ""]
splitter = ___

# TODO: split the docs into chunks (use split_documents, not split_text)
chunks = ___
print(f"Split into {len(chunks)} chunks")

# TODO: create an OpenAIEmbeddings instance using text-embedding-3-small
embeddings = ___

PERSIST_DIR = "./chroma_db"

# TODO: call Chroma.from_documents with the chunks, embeddings,
#       collection_name="rag_mvp", and persist_directory=PERSIST_DIR
vector_store = ___

print(f"Indexed {vector_store._collection.count()} chunks into Chroma")
tasks.solution:
# add to ingest.py (after the loading section from Task 1)
from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
from langchain_chroma import Chroma
from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv()

splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(
    chunk_size=1000,
    chunk_overlap=150,
    separators=["\n\n", "\n", ". ", " ", ""],
    length_function=len,
)

chunks = splitter.split_documents(docs)
print(f"Split into {len(chunks)} chunks")

embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")

PERSIST_DIR = "./chroma_db"

vector_store = Chroma.from_documents(
    documents=chunks,
    embedding=embeddings,
    collection_name="rag_mvp",
    persist_directory=PERSIST_DIR,
)

print(f"Indexed {vector_store._collection.count()} chunks into Chroma")
tasks.explanation: `split_documents` (not `split_text`) is the correct method here because it carries the parent document's `metadata` onto every child chunk. Without this, every chunk loses its `source` and `page` keys and you cannot cite sources later. The `separators` list makes the splitter recursive: it tries blank lines first, then single newlines, then sentence-ending periods, then spaces, and only cuts mid-word as a last resort — this is what keeps semantic units intact. `Chroma.from_documents` both creates the collection and writes all the vectors in one call; the `persist_directory` argument tells Chroma to save to disk so you do not have to re-embed on every run. Run `ingest.py` once, verify the count printed matches roughly (number of docs × average doc length / 1000), then do not run it again until your source documents change.
hints: Use `splitter.split_documents(docs)` not `splitter.split_text(...)`. The former propagates metadata; the latter does not.

If `load_dotenv()` is called before `OpenAIEmbeddings(...)`, it will find the key in `.env` automatically. If you get an `AuthenticationError`, check that `.env` exists in the same folder where you run the script and that the key starts with `sk-`.

After running the script, you should see a `chroma_db/` directory appear in your project folder. If the directory exists but `_collection.count()` returns 0, the `from_documents` call likely failed silently — wrap it in a try/except and print the exception to diagnose.

tasks.title: Core 2: Build the retriever and the full LCEL RAG chain
tasks.prompt: Create `query.py`. Re-open the persisted Chroma collection, build a similarity retriever with `k=4`, write a `format_docs` helper that tags each retrieved chunk with its source file and page number, then assemble a full RAG chain using LCEL (`|` operator) that goes: retriever → format_docs → prompt → LLM → StrOutputParser. Use the system prompt from the lecture that instructs the model to answer only from context and say 'I don't know' if the answer is absent. Finally, wrap the chain in a REPL loop so the user can type questions interactively.
tasks.starter_code:
# query.py
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings, ChatOpenAI
from langchain_chroma import Chroma
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from langchain_core.output_parsers import StrOutputParser
from langchain_core.runnables import RunnablePassthrough
from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv()

embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")

# TODO: re-open the persisted Chroma collection (same collection_name and persist_directory as ingest.py)
vector_store = ___

# TODO: create a retriever with search_type="similarity" and k=4
retriever = ___

# TODO: implement format_docs(docs) that returns a single string
#       where each chunk is prefixed with [source, p.N] and separated by "\n\n---\n\n"
def format_docs(docs):
    pass

llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini", temperature=0)

# TODO: write the ChatPromptTemplate with the "answer only from context" system instruction
prompt = ___

# TODO: assemble the LCEL chain
rag_chain = ___

# TODO: add a REPL loop that reads user input, invokes the chain, and prints the answer
tasks.solution:
# query.py
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings, ChatOpenAI
from langchain_chroma import Chroma
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from langchain_core.output_parsers import StrOutputParser
from langchain_core.runnables import RunnablePassthrough
from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv()

embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")

vector_store = Chroma(
    collection_name="rag_mvp",
    embedding_function=embeddings,
    persist_directory="./chroma_db",
)

retriever = vector_store.as_retriever(
    search_type="similarity",
    search_kwargs={"k": 4},
)

def format_docs(docs):
    formatted = []
    for d in docs:
        src = d.metadata.get("source", "unknown")
        page = d.metadata.get("page")
        tag = f"{src}" + (f", p.{page + 1}" if page is not None else "")
        formatted.append(f"[{tag}]\n{d.page_content}")
    return "\n\n---\n\n".join(formatted)

llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini", temperature=0)

prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_template(
    """You are a careful assistant that answers questions strictly from the provided context.

Rules:
- Use only the information in the context below.
- If the answer is not in the context, say \"I don't know based on the provided documents.\"
- Quote short phrases verbatim when helpful and cite the source filename in parentheses.

Context:
{context}

Question:
{question}

Answer:"""
)

rag_chain = (
    {"context": retriever | format_docs, "question": RunnablePassthrough()}
    | prompt
    | llm
    | StrOutputParser()
)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    print("RAG MVP ready. Type 'exit' to quit.\n")
    while True:
        q = input("You: ").strip()
        if q.lower() in {"exit", "quit"}:
            break
        if not q:
            continue
        print("\nBot:", rag_chain.invoke(q), "\n")
tasks.explanation: The LCEL chain `{"context": retriever | format_docs, "question": RunnablePassthrough()} | prompt | llm | StrOutputParser()` executes as follows: the dict at the start is a `RunnableParallel` — both branches run simultaneously. The left branch sends the question to the retriever, gets back a list of `Document` objects, and pipes them through `format_docs` to produce a context string. The right branch lets the raw question string pass through unchanged via `RunnablePassthrough`. Both values are merged into a dict with keys `context` and `question`, which fills in the two placeholders in the prompt template. The completed prompt goes to the LLM; `StrOutputParser` strips the AIMessage wrapper and returns a plain string. `temperature=0` is essential for factual Q&A: it makes the model deterministic and discourages creative fabrication. The `format_docs` function adds `[source, p.N]` tags before each chunk so the LLM can include proper citations in its answer and so you can trace any claim back to a page in a specific file.
hints: When re-opening an existing Chroma collection use `Chroma(collection_name=..., embedding_function=..., persist_directory=...)` — not `Chroma.from_documents`. The `from_documents` class method creates a new collection (or overwrites the existing one); the constructor opens an existing one.
In `format_docs`, `page` is 0-indexed when it comes from `PyPDFLoader`, so print it as `page + 1` to show the human-readable page number.

If `rag_chain.invoke(q)` raises a `KeyError` about `context` or `question`, check that your prompt template uses exactly those two variable names `{context}` and `{question}` and that the dict keys in the LCEL chain match them exactly.

tasks.title: Applied: Swap the cloud models for a fully local Ollama backend
tasks.prompt: Without changing any loader, splitter, Chroma, retriever, or prompt code, replace the OpenAI LLM and embeddings with Ollama equivalents (`llama3.2` for the chat model, `mxbai-embed-large` for embeddings). Create `ingest_local.py` and `query_local.py` by copying the originals and making only the two lines that differ. Delete the `chroma_db/` folder before running `ingest_local.py` so the new embedding vectors are stored in a clean collection. Verify the REPL still works and answers questions from your documents.
tasks.starter_code:
# ingest_local.py — copy ingest.py and change only the embedding import/instantiation
# (no starter code provided — the exercise tests whether you can isolate the diff)

# query_local.py — copy query.py and change only the LLM and embedding import/instantiation
tasks.solution:
# ingest_local.py  (only the changed lines shown; everything else is identical to ingest.py)
# pip install langchain-ollama
# ollama pull mxbai-embed-large

from langchain_ollama import OllamaEmbeddings   # <-- changed
# ... (all loader and splitter code unchanged) ...

embeddings = OllamaEmbeddings(model="mxbai-embed-large")  # <-- changed

# Chroma.from_documents call is unchanged


# query_local.py  (only the changed lines shown; everything else is identical to query.py)
# ollama pull llama3.2

from langchain_ollama import OllamaEmbeddings, OllamaLLM  # <-- changed

embeddings = OllamaEmbeddings(model="mxbai-embed-large")  # <-- changed
llm = OllamaLLM(model="llama3.2")                         # <-- changed

# retriever, format_docs, prompt, rag_chain, and REPL are all unchanged
tasks.explanation: This task makes the key architectural point concrete: LangChain's abstractions let you swap one component at a time without touching the rest of the pipeline. The only things that must change together are the embedding model in `ingest_local.py` and the embedding model in `query_local.py` — they must stay in sync because vectors produced by `mxbai-embed-large` live in a different geometric space than vectors produced by `text-embedding-3-small`. Deleting `chroma_db/` before re-ingesting ensures you are not mixing vectors from two different embedding models in the same collection (a silent but fatal mistake). The loaders, splitter, retriever, prompt template, and REPL are completely agnostic to which model produced the vectors — that is exactly why the abstraction exists.
hints: Install `langchain-ollama` (`pip install langchain-ollama`) and make sure the Ollama daemon is running (`ollama serve`) before you run the scripts.

You must delete (or rename) the `chroma_db/` folder before running `ingest_local.py`. If the old OpenAI vectors are still there, the retriever will compare `mxbai-embed-large` query vectors against `text-embedding-3-small` document vectors and return meaningless results.

If `OllamaLLM` is not found, check your `langchain-ollama` version. In some versions the class is `ChatOllama` from `langchain_community.chat_models`. Both work; just make sure you import the right one and that the REPL call `rag_chain.invoke(q)` still returns a plain string.

tasks.title: End-to-end sanity check: Retrieval quality and guardrail test
tasks.prompt:
Run two targeted checks to verify the MVP is behaving correctly.

**Check A — Retrieval sanity:** Before invoking the full RAG chain, call `retriever.invoke(question)` directly with a question whose answer you know comes from a specific document. Print all returned chunks (source, page, first 300 characters). Confirm that at least one of the top-4 chunks comes from the expected document.

**Check B — Guardrail test:** Ask the RAG chain a question whose answer is deliberately *not* in any of your documents (e.g., 'What is the boiling point of nitrogen?'). The expected response is a variant of 'I don't know based on the provided documents.' If the chain instead fabricates an answer, identify which part of the prompt controls this and suggest one concrete fix.
tasks.starter_code:
# Add to query.py (or run as a separate test script)

# Check A: inspect raw retrieval results
test_question_a = "<replace with a question whose answer is in your data>"
hits = retriever.invoke(test_question_a)
for i, doc in enumerate(hits, 1):
    print(f"--- Hit {i} (source: {doc.metadata.get('source')}, page: {doc.metadata.get('page')}) ---")
    print(doc.page_content[:300])
    print()

# Check B: guardrail test
test_question_b = "What is the boiling point of nitrogen?"
response_b = rag_chain.invoke(test_question_b)
print("Guardrail test response:", response_b)
tasks.solution:
# Check A — example output interpretation (actual output depends on your data)
# If the retriever returns 4 chunks and at least 1 comes from the file you expect,
# retrieval is working. If none match, the issue is likely:
#   - chunk_size too large (relevant text is buried inside a big chunk)
#   - wrong embedding model used at ingest time vs query time
#   - k is too small (try k=6 or k=8 temporarily)

hits = retriever.invoke(test_question_a)
for i, doc in enumerate(hits, 1):
    print(f"--- Hit {i} (source: {doc.metadata.get('source')}, page: {doc.metadata.get('page')}) ---")
    print(doc.page_content[:300])
    print()

# Check B — expected output:
# "I don't know based on the provided documents."
#
# If the model instead answers correctly from world knowledge, the guardrail is leaking.
# Concrete fixes:
#   1. Make the instruction more explicit:
#      "Do NOT use any knowledge from your training data.
#       If the answer is absent from the context, say exactly:
#       'I don't know based on the provided documents.'"
#   2. Add a second enforcement sentence:
#      "Treat the context as your only source of truth."
#   3. Use a system message (ChatPromptTemplate with SystemMessage) instead of
#      embedding the rule in the human turn — LLMs tend to follow system-role
#      instructions more reliably.

response_b = rag_chain.invoke(test_question_b)
print("Guardrail test response:", response_b)
# A passing result: the response contains 'don't know' or 'not in the' etc.
# A failing result: the model states the boiling point (-195.8 °C) from training data.
tasks.explanation: Running retrieval in isolation (Check A) before blaming the LLM is the single most important diagnostic habit in RAG development. The lecture states it directly: 'If the retriever isn't surfacing the right chunks here, no amount of clever prompting downstream will save you. Fix retrieval first.' Printing the top-k chunks lets you confirm the signal is present before you even involve the LLM. Check B tests the 'confidently wrong' failure mode: a RAG system that ignores the context restriction and draws on parametric (training-time) knowledge will hallucinate on questions that are in its training data but not in your documents. The fix is always in the prompt — either by making the restriction sentence more explicit, adding a second reinforcing sentence, or moving the rule to the system role where models tend to obey it more reliably. `temperature=0` reduces but does not eliminate this risk, which is why the prompt instruction is the primary guardrail.
hints: For Check A, pick a question with a specific, verifiable answer — a policy number, a product name, a date — rather than a vague topic question. Specific questions make it immediately obvious whether the right chunk was retrieved.

For Check B, choose a question that is common world knowledge but genuinely absent from your documents. 'What is the boiling point of nitrogen?' is good because no business document is likely to contain it, so any answer the model gives must be fabricated from training data.

If the guardrail fails (model answers from world knowledge), wrap the 'I don't know' instruction in a separate `SystemMessage` using `ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([('system', '...'), ('human', '{context}\n\nQuestion: {question}')])`. System-role instructions carry more weight than instructions embedded in the human turn.
